rules.name: frontend_integration
rules.overview:
Production-grade pattern for integrating guardrails into a browser-based chat UI. The demo at smart-supplier-selection-demo wires guardrail calls directly from the React frontend to the gov backend — that's a prototype shortcut, not production. This file documents the right pattern.
Read this when the partner is building a chat / form / single-page-app interface that needs guardrails on user input and agent output.

tier_1.name: Browser (chat widget)
tier_1.responsibilities: Render the UI. Generate a per-request correlation ID. Show fallback messages on Block. Show "flagged for review" badges on Flag (if relevant to the partner's UX).
tier_1.must_not: Hold WATSONX_APIKEY. Persist audit data to localStorage. Call the guardrails REST endpoint directly.

tier_2.name: Partner backend (proxy)
tier_2.responsibilities: Authenticate the browser session (cookie / JWT). Call the guardrails service. Write to AuditLogger. Apply per-tenant policy lookup. Implement fail-open / fail-closed logic when guardrails is degraded. Strip / sanitize fields before returning to the browser.

tier_3.name: Guardrails service (REST)
tier_3.responsibilities: Run the 28-metric evaluation. Return scored JSON.

tier_4.name: IBM Cloud (Granite Guardian + watsonx.ai)
tier_4.responsibilities: SaaS-managed by IBM. Partner doesn't operate these.

why_backend_proxy_is_mandatory.reason: WATSONX_APIKEY must never reach the browser. A browser bundle is public; anyone can read it. Direct browser → guardrails-service calls leak the IBM Cloud creds even if the service is private.
Audit log lives server-side. localStorage is not durable, not joinable across users, not compliant with retention regulations, and not tamper-evident.
Per-tenant policy lookup is a backend concern. The browser doesn't know — and shouldn't know — that tenant Acme has different PII thresholds than tenant Globex.
Fail-open / fail-closed decisions vary per partner. Backend can fall back to "allow with flag" when guardrails is down; browser can't make that policy call.

chat_widget_event_handler_pattern.description: The demo at /Users/shima/src/ibm-internal-github/smart-supplier-selection-demo/smart-supplier-selection-demo/app/src/components/home.jsx wires guardrail calls at two specific moments in the chat lifecycle. We replicate the pattern but route through our own backend.

input_check.when: User clicks Send (or presses Enter) in the chat input.
input_check.flow: 1. Generate request_id (UUID). 2. POST to YOUR_BACKEND/chat with {query, request_id}. 3. Backend proxies query → guardrails service for INPUT check. 4. If guardrails returns Block: backend returns {action: "Block", fallback_message: "..."}. 5. If Pass/Flag: backend calls the agent, then runs OUTPUT check, then returns the response. 6. Frontend renders fallback message on Block; renders agent response (with optional Flag badge) on Pass/Flag.

output_check.handler: Backend-side, before returning to browser (NOT browser-side).
output_check.reason: Output checks have to look at the LLM's response. The cleanest place to do this is the backend, in the same request cycle that called the agent. Browser-side output checks (like the demo's instance.on('receive')) are a leak vector and harder to make idempotent.

correlation_id_propagation.generate: Frontend generates `request_id = crypto.randomUUID()` once per user message.
correlation_id_propagation.propagate: Send as a header `X-Request-ID` and in the JSON body. Backend reads it, passes to AuditLogger via `request_id=`, and echoes back to the browser.
correlation_id_propagation.use: Frontend can later query audit log by request_id if user disputes a refusal.

action.name: Pass
action.ui: Show the agent response normally. No badge, no extra UI.

action.name: Flag
action.ui: Show the agent response. Optionally show a subtle "flagged for review" badge IF the partner's policy is to surface borderline content to the user. Many partners hide the Flag state from end users and only surface it in the compliance dashboard.

action.name: Block
action.ui: Replace the agent response with `fallback_message`. Don't tell the user which metric tripped (that's adversarial intel). Optionally provide a "try rephrasing" hint.

anti_pattern.name: Direct browser-to-guardrails calls
localStorage for audit trail
anti_pattern.bad: // BAD: audit data in localStorage — not durable, not compliant localStorage.setItem('watsonTransactions', JSON.stringify([...prev, decision]));
anti_pattern.good: Audit lives server-side via AuditLogger. localStorage is fine for UX state (e.g. last-N visible messages) but NEVER for compliance-grade records.

anti_pattern.name: Exposing internal metric names to end users
anti_pattern.good: "Your request couldn't be processed. Please rephrase your question." (the metric.fallback_message)
anti_pattern.reason: Telling adversarial users which metric they tripped helps them iterate around it.

reference_files.file: .bob/reference-payloads/backend_guardrails_proxy.py — Flask backend proxy (used by both D.2 and D.3)
